Where fragmentation actually lives

Every organization is quietly losing the same things —
in different shapes.

Fragmentation is not abstract. It is a specific set of artifacts, scattered across a specific set of tools, paying a specific monthly tax. Pick an audience. See what they actually carry, scattered.

View
Audience 01 · Movement leader

A body of work, dispersed across thirty years of platforms.

Books across publishers. Talks on platforms you don't own. Manuscripts in five tools. Email archives that hold the actual network. Use the sticky controls above to switch between the informational field and the relational one — the same fragmented life in two shapes.

Publisher 5 active
BP

Brazos · Bob Hosack

Editor since 2006

Royalties Q3open
The reality

The body of work lives in files, platforms, and inboxes that never speak to one another. What feels like being everywhere is, structurally, nowhere unified — so nothing can rank it, cite it, translate it, or pass it on as a whole.

The cost

Decades of authority — distributed across too many platforms to rank, ground, or carry forward.

  • SEO. Your canonical articulation of a concept you've taught for thirty years doesn't exist as a URL.
  • GEO. AI cites competitors' versions of your frameworks because theirs are cleaner and more attributable.
  • Succession. The relational network that carries the work lives in your inbox and your head.
  • AI tooling. Anything trying to "speak in your voice" hallucinates — the corpus isn't groundable.
  • Translation. A scattered corpus is prohibitive to translate. You're effectively English-only.
  • Calendar capacity. You are the network's only memory. Every warm relationship has to touch you.
Audience 02 · Nonprofit

Two halves of one mission, on two halves of an unintegrated stack.

Programs generate stories. Development generates funding. The two operate side-by-side without a shared foundation. The taxes show up as silently lapsed donors, grant rewrite hours, and case studies nobody can find at the moment of need. Switch the field above to compare informational surfaces with relational ones.

StatusRenewal Q4
The reality

Programs and development each tell a true story of the mission — from different tools, to different audiences, with no shared ground. Donors, grant officers, and beneficiaries meet parallel versions of the same organization.

The cost

Programs and development each running at half their potential — because they don't share a foundation.

  • Lapsed donors. The single largest revenue leak — they didn't leave, they weren't seen.
  • Grant rewrites. Hours spent re-articulating what you've already articulated, dozens of times a year.
  • Program ↔ development split. The stories programs generate never reach the people who could fund them.
  • SEO invisibility. Substantive work trapped in PDFs and closed newsletters; newcomers can't find you.
  • Staff turnover. Every two years a development hire walks out with the relational memory.
  • Lost storytelling. The case study that would carry every appeal sits in a comms folder no one searches.
Audience 03 · Church

Decades of preaching, formation, and care — most of which serves one Sunday.

The pastor invests fifteen hours in a sermon and posts audio. The ChMS knows attendance and giving — not the seasons of life or formation trajectory. Care begins again with each staff transition. Use the field toggle above to contrast teaching artifacts with pastoral and volunteer relationships.

JL

Jay L. · former intern

Now planting · Oakland

Network0 carriers
The reality

Weekend expression, pastoral care, and digital comms rarely share a spine. The congregation meets one church; the systems reflect many — so teaching doesn't compound, care doesn't carry, and the story resets with every turnover.

The cost

A church that survives decades but goes amnesiac about its own people, teaching, and story.

  • Sermons that serve one Sunday. Fifteen-hour investments that almost no one will ever find again.
  • Formation without knowledge of the formed. Discipleship offered to a congregation the church can't actually see.
  • Pastoral discontinuity. Care that began for a long-time member begins again from zero with each staff transition.
  • Guest pipeline leakage. People who would flourish drop off in seams only visible in aggregate.
  • SEO/GEO invisibility. Real theological questions are answered by everyone but you.
  • Volunteer burnout. The same people serve in four ministries because no one sees the load across them.
  • Lost institutional memory. The church's own story carried by people who eventually leave.
Audience 04 · Seminary / institution

An institution's most valuable assets — alumni, faculty work, research — silently under-stewarded.

The same person appears in five systems with five partial pictures. Faculty research the institution paid for ranks below aggregators. Advancement carries the real picture in heads and personal notes — which depart with each staff turnover. The toggle above separates research and curriculum artifacts from donor, partner, and alumni relationships.

Seoul Theol. Univ.

Visiting scholar pgm.

MOU yr2027
The reality

Scholarship, formation, and advancement run on different rails with different owners. The graduate meets a fractured institution long before they meet a coherent one — and the same human shows up as five partial records across the stack.

The cost

An institution paying for research it cannot find and stewarding alumni it cannot see.

  • Silo duplication. The same person — alum, donor, parent, partner — appears in five systems with five partial pictures.
  • Advancement flying blind. Major-gift officers carry the real picture in heads and personal notes, lost on turnover.
  • Research that cannot be found. You pay for it; competitors get cited for the topics it addresses.
  • Alumni invisibility. Your most significant relational asset, almost universally under-stewarded.
  • Accreditation costs. The self-study takes hundreds of hours largely because the data is fragmented.
  • AI that embarrasses. Chatbots can't answer substantive questions about the institution's own programs or faculty.
  • Recruitment leakage. Promising candidates fall through gaps across systems and staff.
Where this goes next

Every audience above carries the same problem in a different shape. The repair is the same shape, too.

Naming the artifacts is the first move. The second is integration: typed content, related people, one source of truth — and many expressions. That's the next page.
